facts.text: Depeche Mode has sold over 100 million records worldwide, making them one of the most commercially successful electronic bands ever.
The Roland Juno-106 polysynth, released in 1984, became one of the most widely used synthesizers in new wave and synth-pop production.
Joy Division's 'Love Will Tear Us Apart' was released as a single on June 18, 1980, just over a month after Ian Curtis's death.
Blondie was one of the first American new wave bands to achieve mainstream success, blending punk, disco, and pop influences.
The Yamaha DX7, released in 1983, used FM synthesis and became the best-selling synthesizer of all time with over 200,000 units sold.
Gary Numan's 'Cars' reached number one in the UK in 1979 and became an anthem of the emerging synth-pop movement.
Factory Records, founded in Manchester in 1978, became home to Joy Division and New Order, defining the post-punk and new wave sound.
The New Romantic movement emerged from London's Blitz nightclub in the early 1980s, championing flamboyant fashion and synthesizer-driven music.
Kraftwerk's 'The Man-Machine' (1978) and 'Computer World' (1981) were foundational influences on virtually every synth-pop act that followed.
A-ha's 'Take On Me' music video pioneered a rotoscoping technique that combined pencil-sketch animation with live action.
David Bowie's 'Berlin Trilogy' — 'Low', 'Heroes', and 'Lodger' — recorded with Brian Eno, laid groundwork for the new wave and synth-pop genres.
MTV launched on August 1, 1981, with the Buggles' 'Video Killed the Radio Star' as its first music video.
The Roland TR-808 drum machine, originally considered a commercial failure, became a cornerstone of electronic music production.
Tears for Fears took their name from Arthur Janov's primal therapy theories, which band members Roland Orzabal and Curt Smith had studied.
Siouxsie and the Banshees released 11 studio albums between 1978 and 1995, influencing gothic rock, new wave, and alternative music.
The Cure's Robert Smith is known for his signature look of smeared red lipstick, wild black hair, and oversized black clothing.
Pet Shop Boys' 'West End Girls' topped charts in the UK, US, and multiple other countries in 1985-86, blending synth-pop with hip-hop influences.
The Fairlight CMI (Computer Musical Instrument), first produced in 1979, was an early digital sampler used by Kate Bush, Peter Gabriel, and Duran Duran.
Echo & the Bunnymen's debut album 'Crocodiles' (1980) featured a drum machine credited as a band member named 'Echo.'
Soft Cell's 'Tainted Love' spent 43 weeks on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1981-82, setting a record at the time for the longest chart run.
The Sequential Circuits Prophet-5 was the first fully programmable polyphonic synthesizer and was used extensively by new wave artists.
Duran Duran filmed their 'Hungry Like the Wolf' video in Sri Lanka, helping establish the trend of exotic, cinematic music videos.
Wire's albums 'Pink Flag' (1977), 'Chairs Missing' (1978), and '154' (1979) are considered essential proto-new wave records.
Thomas Dolby's 'She Blinded Me with Science' reached number five on the US Billboard Hot 100 in 1983 despite being a quirky synth-pop novelty.
The term 'new wave' was originally used interchangeably with 'punk rock' before evolving to describe a more pop-oriented, synthesizer-driven sound.
New Order's 'Blue Monday,' released on March 7, 1983, became the best-selling 12-inch single of all time.
Bauhaus's debut single 'Bela Lugosi's Dead' (1979) is often cited as the first gothic rock record, running over nine minutes long.
The B-52s combined surf rock, punk, and new wave with kitschy aesthetics, becoming one of the most distinctive bands of the early 1980s.
Yellow Magic Orchestra from Japan released 'Solid State Survivor' in 1979, influencing synth-pop and early electronic dance music worldwide.
The Korg MS-20, a semi-modular monophonic synthesizer released in 1978, became a favorite of experimental new wave and post-punk musicians.
Martin Hannett, Factory Records' house producer, was known for eccentric studio techniques like recording sounds in elevators and using unusual reverb effects.
Human League scored a massive hit with 'Don't You (Forget About Me)' — actually, that was Simple Minds; Human League's biggest hit was 'Don't You Want Me' in 1981.
Cyndi Lauper's 'She's So Unusual' (1983) made her the first female artist to have four top-five singles from a debut album.
The Moog synthesizer, developed by Robert Moog in the 1960s, paved the way for every electronic music genre that followed.
CBGB in New York City, primarily known as a punk venue, also hosted early new wave acts like Talking Heads, Blondie, and the Ramones.
Ultravox's 'Vienna' was kept from the UK number one spot in 1981 by Joe Dolce's novelty hit 'Shaddap You Face,' a fact that still frustrates fans.
Gang of Four's 'Entertainment!' (1979) blended angular guitar riffs with Marxist lyrics, becoming a landmark of post-punk and new wave.
The Roland Jupiter-8, released in 1981, was an eight-voice polyphonic analog synthesizer favored by Duran Duran and Howard Jones.
Kate Bush became the first female artist to have a self-written song reach number one in the UK with 'Wuthering Heights' in 1978.
Erasure, the duo of Andy Bell and Vince Clarke, has had over 30 UK Top 40 singles since forming in 1985.
Russell Mulcahy directed some of the most iconic new wave music videos, including Duran Duran's 'Wild Boys' and Bonnie Tyler's 'Total Eclipse of the Heart.'
The LinnDrum, introduced in 1982, was one of the first drum machines to use digital samples of real drums and appeared on countless new wave records.
Talking Heads' 'Remain in Light' (1980) drew heavily from African polyrhythmic music, Fela Kuti, and Brian Eno's ambient production techniques.
4AD Records, founded in 1980, released music by Cocteau Twins, Dead Can Dance, and Pixies, cultivating an atmospheric, ethereal aesthetic.
The Oberheim OB-Xa synthesizer was used prominently on Van Halen's 'Jump,' but also appeared on countless new wave and synth-pop records.
Depeche Mode's 'Violator' (1990) featured 'Personal Jesus' and 'Enjoy the Silence,' becoming a landmark album that bridged synth-pop and alternative rock.
Billy Idol began his career as the guitarist of Generation X before launching a hugely successful solo career blending punk attitude with synth-pop production.
DAF (Deutsch Amerikanische Freundschaft) were pioneers of Electronic Body Music (EBM), blending minimal synths with aggressive vocals from Düsseldorf.
New Order's Peter Hook developed a distinctive bass guitar style, playing melodic lines high on the neck of his Yamaha BB1200 bass.
CHVRCHES, formed in Glasgow in 2011, are one of the most prominent modern acts carrying on the synth-pop tradition.
The Rickenbacker bass guitar, favored by post-punk bassists like Peter Hook of New Order, contributed a bright, cutting tone to many new wave records.
Conny Plank, the German producer and engineer, worked with Kraftwerk, DAF, Ultravox, and Eurythmics, shaping the sound of European electronic music.
Magazine, fronted by Howard Devoto after he left Buzzcocks, released 'Real Life' in 1978, one of the earliest post-punk albums.
The chorus pedal effect, particularly the Roland CE-1 and Boss CE-2, became synonymous with the jangly, shimmering guitar tones of new wave.
Tears for Fears' 'Songs from the Big Chair' (1985) contained both 'Shout' and 'Everybody Wants to Rule the World,' two of the decade's biggest hits.
INXS emerged from Australia's new wave scene, blending funk, dance-rock, and synth-pop to become one of the biggest bands of the late 1980s.
Vince Clarke has been a founding member of three major synth-pop acts: Depeche Mode, Yazoo, and Erasure.
Howard Jones had three UK Top 10 hits in 1984 alone: 'New Song,' 'What Is Love?,' and 'Hide and Seek.'
Einstürzende Neubauten, formed in West Berlin in 1980, used power tools, metal pipes, and industrial debris as musical instruments.
The flanger effect, used extensively in new wave production, creates a swirling, jet-engine sound by combining two identical signals with a slight delay.
Depeche Mode was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2020 after decades of influence on electronic and alternative music.
The Cure's 'Disintegration' (1989) is often considered their masterpiece, featuring lush synth layers, reverb-drenched guitars, and emotionally intense lyrics.
Icehouse (originally Flowers) were one of Australia's most successful new wave bands, known for hits like 'Electric Blue' and 'Great Southern Land.'
Synth-pop's visual aesthetic often included angular haircuts, heavy eyeliner, leather jackets, and pastel or neon color palettes.
M83, the French electronic project led by Anthony Gonzalez, has drawn heavily from 1980s synth-pop, especially on albums like 'Hurry Up, We're Dreaming.'
Gary Numan was born Gary Anthony James Webb on March 8, 1958, in Hammersmith, London.
The Roland TR-909, released in 1983, combined analog and digital sounds and later became foundational to house and techno music.
Blondie's 'Heart of Glass' (1979) shocked punk purists by incorporating disco elements with new wave, topping charts worldwide.
Tim Pope directed most of The Cure's music videos, creating surreal, darkly humorous visuals that became inseparable from the band's identity.
Yazoo (known as Yaz in the US) consisted of Vince Clarke and Alison Moyet; they released only two albums before splitting in 1983.
The 1975, formed in Manchester, have cited new wave and synth-pop as primary influences on their sound and visual aesthetic.
Power shoulders and oversized blazers became fashion staples during the new wave era, symbolizing bold confidence and futuristic style.
Talking Heads' concert film 'Stop Making Sense' (1984), directed by Jonathan Demme, is widely regarded as one of the greatest concert films ever made.
Joy Division's Ian Curtis died on May 18, 1980, at the age of 23, on the eve of the band's first American tour.
The Rough Trade record shop, opened in London in 1976, grew into Rough Trade Records, releasing music by The Smiths, Stiff Little Fingers, and many post-punk acts.
The synthwave revival of the 2010s, with artists like Kavinsky, Perturbator, and Carpenter Brut, drew direct inspiration from 1980s synth-pop and film scores.
OMD (Orchestral Manoeuvres in the Dark) scored a worldwide hit with 'Enola Gay' in 1980, a synth-pop song about the atomic bombing of Hiroshima.
The Cure's Robert Smith was born on April 21, 1959, in Blackpool, Lancashire, England.
Duran Duran were one of the first bands to fully exploit MTV, using lavish, cinematic music videos to build a massive global fanbase.
New Order combined post-punk guitar work with electronic dance beats, essentially inventing a template for alternative dance music.
Siouxsie Sioux's striking visual style — heavy eye makeup, jet-black hair, bold fashion — became a defining image of post-punk and goth culture.
The Mute Records label, founded by Daniel Miller in 1978, became home to Depeche Mode, Erasure, Yazoo, and Nick Cave.
Kate Bush's 'Running Up That Hill' experienced a massive resurgence in 2022 after being featured in the TV series 'Stranger Things.'
Soft Cell's Marc Almond went on to a prolific solo career, releasing over 20 solo albums after the duo's initial split in 1984.
The B-52s' self-titled debut album (1979) featured the party anthem 'Rock Lobster' and showcased their camp, retro-futuristic style.
Human League's Phil Oakey recruited schoolgirls Joanne Catherall and Susan Ann Sulley from a Sheffield nightclub to join the band as vocalists.
A-ha were formed in Oslo, Norway, in 1982 by Morten Harket, Magne Furuholmen, and Pål Waaktaar-Savoy.
The Manchester scene of the late 1970s and early 1980s produced Joy Division, New Order, The Smiths, and later the 'Madchester' movement.
David Bowie released 'Heroes' on October 14, 1977, recorded at Hansa Studios near the Berlin Wall.
Vaporwave, emerging in the early 2010s, drew from 1980s synth-pop, smooth jazz, and corporate muzak to create an ironic, nostalgic aesthetic.
The Cure has released 13 studio albums over more than four decades, spanning post-punk, gothic rock, synth-pop, and dream pop.
Depeche Mode's Alan Wilder, who joined in 1982, was crucial to the band's sonic evolution before departing in 1995.
New wave fashion often featured skinny ties, wraparound sunglasses, and geometric patterns in bold primary colors.
Pet Shop Boys' Neil Tennant previously worked as a journalist for Smash Hits magazine before becoming one of synth-pop's biggest stars.
The Roland Juno-60, predecessor to the Juno-106, featured a built-in arpeggiator and warm analog pads beloved by new wave keyboardists.
Echo & the Bunnymen's Ian McCulloch was known for his dramatic vocal style and brooding stage presence, earning comparisons to Jim Morrison.
Duran Duran's 'Rio' (1982) became an iconic new wave album, with its title track featuring Simon Le Bon's soaring vocals over Nick Rhodes' layered synths.
Bauhaus disbanded in 1983 but their influence on goth, post-punk, and dark wave continued to reverberate for decades.
INXS frontman Michael Hutchence was born on January 22, 1960, in Sydney, Australia.
Gary Numan's 'The Pleasure Principle' (1979) was notable for being an album recorded without any guitar, relying entirely on synthesizers.
The flanger and chorus effects units made by Boss and Roland became essential pedalboard staples for new wave guitarists.
Tears for Fears' Roland Orzabal and Curt Smith grew up together in Bath, England, and had a famously volatile creative partnership.
Blondie's Debbie Harry was one of the most recognizable faces of the new wave era, appearing on magazine covers worldwide.
The Fairlight CMI cost around $25,000 in 1979 — roughly $100,000 in today's money — making it accessible only to top-tier artists and producers.
New Order's Bernard Sumner taught himself to play guitar and synthesizer after the death of Ian Curtis forced Joy Division to reinvent themselves.
Simple Minds' 'Don't You (Forget About Me)' was written by Keith Forsey for the soundtrack of 'The Breakfast Club' (1985).
The gated reverb drum sound, popularized by Phil Collins and Hugh Padgham, became a signature sonic element of 1980s new wave and pop production.
Erasure's Andy Bell was one of the first openly gay pop stars in the UK, coming out publicly in 1986.
Depeche Mode's 'Personal Jesus' (1989) has been covered by Johnny Cash, Marilyn Manson, and numerous other artists across genres.
Wire's 'Pink Flag' (1977) contained 21 tracks in just 35 minutes, with some songs lasting under a minute, influencing hardcore punk and new wave alike.
The Cure released 'Just Like Heaven' as a single in 1987; it became one of the most beloved songs in alternative music history.
A-ha's 'Take On Me' music video was directed by Steve Barron and took 16 weeks to complete due to the complex animation.
Yellow Magic Orchestra's Ryuichi Sakamoto went on to become an Academy Award-winning film composer for 'The Last Emperor' (1987).
Thomas Dolby later became a technology entrepreneur, founding Beatnik Inc., which developed audio technology for mobile phones.
The Talking Heads were formed at the Rhode Island School of Design in 1975 by David Byrne, Chris Frantz, and Tina Weymouth.
Duran Duran was named after a character in the 1968 science fiction film 'Barbarella,' starring Jane Fonda.
The Cure's 'Boys Don't Cry' was released on June 15, 1979, and became one of post-punk's most enduring anthems.
Billy Idol's 'White Wedding' was released in 1982 and featured a driving synth-punk energy that became his signature sound.
Depeche Mode began as a bubbly synth-pop act but evolved into a darker, more industrial-influenced sound through the mid-to-late 1980s.
Kate Bush wrote 'Wuthering Heights' when she was just 18 years old, inspired by a BBC television adaptation of the Emily Brontë novel.
The Roland TB-303, originally designed as a bass accompaniment tool, was adopted by new wave and later acid house musicians for its squelchy sound.
Siouxsie and the Banshees' 'Juju' (1981) is considered one of the most important post-punk albums, blending dark atmospherics with tribal rhythms.
Pet Shop Boys have had more UK Top 40 hits than any other duo in chart history.
The post-punk movement emerged in the late 1970s as a reaction to punk's simplicity, incorporating art-rock, electronic, and experimental elements.
New Order's 'Technique' (1989) was recorded in Ibiza, absorbing the island's acid house and balearic beat scenes into their sound.
Depeche Mode's Martin Gore wrote the vast majority of the band's songs, despite Dave Gahan being the primary vocalist.
Cyndi Lauper's 'Girls Just Want to Have Fun' was originally written and recorded by Robert Hazard as a male-perspective song.
The Cure's 'Lovesong' was written by Robert Smith as a wedding gift for his wife Mary, reaching number two on the US Billboard Hot 100 in 1989.
Kraftwerk's Ralf Hütter and Florian Schneider met at a musical improvisation course in Düsseldorf in 1968.
Ultravox's Midge Ure co-wrote and co-produced 'Do They Know It's Christmas?' with Bob Geldof for Band Aid in 1984.
Human League's 'Don't You Want Me' hit number one in both the UK and the US in 1981-82, becoming a synth-pop anthem.
The Cocteau Twins' Elizabeth Fraser developed a distinctive vocal style often described as glossolalia — singing in invented, non-lexical syllables.
New wave's influence on fashion extended to the runway, with designers like Vivienne Westwood and Jean Paul Gaultier incorporating punk and new wave elements.
The Smiths, while primarily guitar-based, emerged from the same Manchester post-punk scene that birthed Joy Division and New Order.
Depeche Mode's 'Music for the Masses' tour culminated in a concert at the Rose Bowl in Pasadena on June 18, 1988, attended by over 60,000 fans.
Gary Numan's 'Are Friends Electric?' topped the UK charts in 1979, launching the synth-pop revolution.
Orchestral Manoeuvres in the Dark were formed in 1978 in Wirral, Merseyside, by Andy McCluskey and Paul Humphreys.
The eyeliner-heavy makeup worn by male new wave artists challenged gender norms and became a defining visual element of the era.
Talking Heads' 'Psycho Killer' (1977) was one of the first songs to emerge from the New York CBGB scene that crossed into mainstream consciousness.
Joy Division's 'Unknown Pleasures' album cover, designed by Peter Saville, depicted stacked radio pulsar waves and became one of the most iconic album covers ever.
A-ha's Morten Harket was born on September 14, 1959, in Kongsberg, Norway.
The Cure's concert at Sydney Opera House in 2019 was one of the band's most acclaimed live performances of the 21st century.
Duran Duran's Nick Rhodes was known for his flamboyant fashion, often wearing heavy makeup and elaborate hairstyles on stage and in videos.
OMD's 'Electricity' (1979) was one of the earliest pure synth-pop singles, released on Factory Records before the band moved to DinDisc.
Soft Cell released 'Tainted Love' on July 1, 1981; it was originally a 1964 Northern Soul song by Gloria Jones.
The Prophet-5 synthesizer's ability to store and recall patches made it revolutionary for live performance and studio work.
Blondie's 'Rapture' (1981) was the first number-one song on the US Billboard Hot 100 to feature rap vocals.
New Order's Stephen Morris was one of the first rock drummers to integrate electronic drum pads and sequencers into his live kit.
Gang of Four's Andy Gill developed a slashing, angular guitar style that influenced bands from Red Hot Chili Peppers to Bloc Party.
Kate Bush's 'Hounds of Love' (1985) is frequently ranked among the greatest albums of all time by music critics.
The pastel color palette of the 1980s — mint green, soft pink, lavender — became closely associated with new wave visual aesthetics.
Pet Shop Boys' 'It's a Sin' referenced Neil Tennant's strict Catholic upbringing and teenage guilt.
Bauhaus's Peter Murphy later had a successful solo career, with 'Cuts You Up' becoming a number one US Modern Rock hit in 1990.
The Cure performed their first concert on December 14, 1976, as 'Easy Cure' at a school party in Crawley, West Sussex.
Tears for Fears' 'Mad World' was covered by Gary Jules for the 'Donnie Darko' soundtrack, reaching number one in the UK in 2003.
Depeche Mode's Dave Gahan struggled with heroin addiction throughout the 1990s, at one point being clinically dead for two minutes after an overdose.
Howard Jones performed solo concerts with just a keyboard rig and a mime artist named Jed in his early career.
The Yamaha CS-80, used by Vangelis on the 'Blade Runner' soundtrack, was a massive polyphonic synthesizer weighing over 200 pounds.
Erasure's 'A Little Respect' (1988) became one of the most recognized synth-pop songs of the decade.
Echo & the Bunnymen's Will Sergeant was known for his extensive use of effects pedals, creating a lush, psychedelic guitar sound.
New Order famously lost money on every copy of 'Blue Monday' sold because the elaborate die-cut sleeve designed by Peter Saville cost more to produce than the record's sale price.
Siouxsie and the Banshees were one of the first punk-era bands to perform on BBC Television, appearing on the 'Today' show in 1977.
The Cure's 'Seventeen Seconds' (1980) established their atmospheric, minimalist sound, with songs like 'A Forest' becoming fan favorites.
Kraftwerk performed entirely with electronic instruments and were among the first to use custom-built electronic percussion in live shows.
Cyndi Lauper won the inaugural Grammy Award for Best New Artist at the 27th Annual Grammy Awards on February 26, 1985.
Duran Duran's John Taylor and Andy Taylor (no relation) formed one of new wave's most dynamic rhythm sections.
The Moog Minimoog Model D, released in 1970, was the first portable synthesizer and appeared on countless new wave and synth-pop recordings.
Talking Heads dissolved in 1991, and despite periodic rumors, they have never officially reunited for a tour or new album.
M83's 'Midnight City' (2011) featured a saxophone solo that became an unlikely indie anthem, recalling 1980s synth-pop grandeur.
Depeche Mode's 'Enjoy the Silence' was originally conceived by Martin Gore as a slow ballad before the band reworked it into an uptempo synth-pop classic.
The B-52s' Fred Schneider's talk-singing vocal style was unique in new wave, adding a campy, theatrical element to the band's sound.
Joy Division's 'Closer' (1980) was released on July 18, 1980, two months after Ian Curtis's death, and is considered one of post-punk's finest albums.
Billy Idol's signature sneer and bleached-blond spiked hair made him one of the most visually recognizable figures of the new wave era.
Gary Numan was diagnosed with Asperger syndrome as an adult, which he credits with fueling his intense focus on music and technology.
The Roland SH-101, a monophonic synthesizer released in 1982, came with an optional handgrip for keytar-style performance on stage.
New Order's 'Substance' compilation (1987) collected all their 12-inch singles and remains one of the best-selling compilation albums in indie music history.
Depeche Mode were originally called 'Composition of Sound' before Vince Clarke joined and the group adopted their famous name.
The name 'Depeche Mode' was taken from a French fashion magazine title meaning 'fast fashion' or 'fashion news.'
Peter Saville, the graphic designer for Factory Records, created iconic artwork for Joy Division and New Order that became as famous as the music itself.
Tears for Fears split in 1991 due to tensions between Orzabal and Smith; they reunited in 2001 and have continued intermittently since.
Human League were originally an experimental electronic duo before splitting into two groups: the pop-oriented Human League and the more avant-garde Heaven 17.
The Cure's 'Friday I'm in Love' was released on May 11, 1992, becoming one of their most commercially successful singles.
Duran Duran's Simon Le Bon nearly drowned during the 1985 Fastnet yacht race when his boat capsized in the Irish Sea.
David Bowie's 'Low' (1977), produced by Tony Visconti with Brian Eno, featured an entire instrumental second side that influenced ambient and electronic music.
The Rickenbacker 4003 bass was favored by many post-punk and new wave bassists for its punchy, trebly tone that cut through synthesizer-heavy mixes.
Blondie's 'Parallel Lines' (1978) is one of the best-selling new wave albums of all time, with over 20 million copies sold worldwide.
OMD's 'If You Leave' was written and recorded in just three and a half hours for the 'Pretty in Pink' film soundtrack in 1986.
The new wave movement largely faded from the mainstream by the late 1980s, supplanted by grunge, hip-hop, and other emerging genres.
Gary Numan's love of science fiction, particularly Philip K. Dick and J.G. Ballard, heavily influenced his lyrics and visual presentation.
Pet Shop Boys produced Dusty Springfield's comeback hit 'What Have I Done to Deserve This?' in 1987, pairing synth-pop with classic pop vocals.
Echo & the Bunnymen's 'Ocean Rain' (1984) was described by Ian McCulloch as 'the greatest album ever made,' reflecting his legendary self-confidence.
The Korg Polysix, released in 1981, was Korg's first fully programmable polyphonic synthesizer and was used by many new wave keyboardists.
Talking Heads' David Byrne wore an oversized suit in the 'Stop Making Sense' concert film, creating one of rock music's most iconic visual moments.
Siouxsie and the Banshees' 'The Scream' (1978) is punk-rooted but layered with atmospherics that presaged post-punk and goth.
Depeche Mode's 'Black Celebration' (1986) marked the band's full transition from pop-synth to darker, more industrial-tinged electronic music.
The new wave and synth-pop movement was closely tied to the rise of affordable synthesizer technology in the late 1970s and early 1980s.
New Order's Gillian Gilbert became one of the most prominent female instrumentalists in electronic music during the 1980s.
The Cure's 'Pornography' (1982) was so bleak and intense that it nearly led to the band's dissolution and Robert Smith's mental breakdown.
Kate Bush performed 'Running Up That Hill' live for the first time in 35 years during her 'Before the Dawn' residency at the Hammersmith Apollo in 2014.
A-ha's Magne Furuholmen is also a successful visual artist, having exhibited paintings and installations in galleries across Europe.
Duran Duran formed in Birmingham, England, in 1978, taking their name from a villain in the Jane Fonda film 'Barbarella.'
Joy Division's producer Martin Hannett insisted on recording in unconventional ways, once making drummer Stephen Morris play his kit one piece at a time.
The synthesizer-heavy soundtrack of 'Blade Runner' (1982), composed by Vangelis, became one of the most influential film scores in sci-fi history.
Soft Cell reunited in 2001 for a tour and released their final album, '*Happiness Not Included,' in 2022.
The Talking Heads' 'Once in a Lifetime' featured David Byrne's jerky, improvisational choreography, inspired by televangelists and ecstatic religious rituals.
Blondie were inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame on March 12, 2006.
Erasure's 'Sometimes' (1986) reached number two in the UK and became one of the most enduring synth-pop ballads of the decade.
The Cure's lineup has changed extensively, with Robert Smith being the only constant member across the band's entire career.
New Order's 'World in Motion' (1990) was the official song of the England football team for the FIFA World Cup, featuring a rap by footballer John Barnes.
Depeche Mode's 'Songs of Faith and Devotion' (1993) introduced live drums, blues guitar, and gospel elements into their electronic sound.
Japan, led by David Sylvian, were one of the most sophisticated new wave acts, blending art-rock, electronic music, and Eastern influences.
The ASCII art and geometric graphics of early MTV bumpers were influenced by the angular visual aesthetic of new wave culture.
Pet Shop Boys' 'Actually' (1987) contained four hit singles and showcased their gift for melding intellectual lyrics with irresistible pop hooks.
Siouxsie Sioux was born Susan Janet Ballion on May 27, 1957, in London, England.
Gary Numan flew across the Atlantic in a small propeller plane in 1981 as part of a round-the-world flight, crash-landing in India.
The sampler revolutionized new wave production, allowing artists to incorporate found sounds, orchestral hits, and vocal snippets into electronic arrangements.
Human League's 'Dare' (1981) was produced by Martin Rushent, who helped create a polished, pop-friendly synth sound that defined early synth-pop.
Wire released their fourth album 'The Ideal Copy' in 1987 after a five-year hiatus, incorporating more electronic and ambient elements.
The Cure's Robert Smith played guitar for both The Cure and Siouxsie and the Banshees simultaneously in 1983, an unusual dual commitment.
Cyndi Lauper's eccentric fashion — mismatched colors, plastic bangles, dyed hair — became a symbol of 1980s new wave individuality.
The original 'Blue Monday' 12-inch single had no title printed on its sleeve, only a color-coded design by Peter Saville.
Depeche Mode's massive following in Eastern Europe and Russia made them cultural icons behind the Iron Curtain during the Cold War.
New Order's 'Power, Corruption & Lies' (1983) blended post-punk atmospherics with burgeoning electronic dance influences.
Thomas Dolby's stage name was inspired by the Dolby noise reduction system, leading to a lawsuit from Dolby Laboratories.
The B-52s took a long hiatus after guitarist Ricky Wilson died of AIDS-related illness in 1985, returning with 'Cosmic Thing' in 1989.
Tears for Fears' 'Everybody Wants to Rule the World' won the Brit Award for Best British Single in 1986.
A-ha's 'Take On Me' was initially released in 1984 with minimal success; a re-release with the famous animated video in 1985 made it a worldwide smash.
Duran Duran's 'Girls on Film' video, directed by Godley & Creme, was banned by the BBC for its provocative content.
Joy Division took their name from 'House of Dolls,' a 1955 novella about forced prostitution in Nazi concentration camps, sparking controversy.
The DX7's electric piano preset became so ubiquitous in the mid-1980s that it is one of the most recognizable synthesizer sounds in pop music history.
New Order were one of the first rock bands to work with hip-hop producer Arthur Baker, on their single 'Confusion' (1983).
The Cure's 'Kiss Me, Kiss Me, Kiss Me' (1987) was a sprawling double album that showcased the band's range from pop to psychedelia to noise.
Ultravox's 'Vienna' (1980) featured Midge Ure's emotive vocals over atmospheric synths and is considered a landmark synth-pop single.
David Bowie's 'Ashes to Ashes' (1980) revisited the character Major Tom from 'Space Oddity' and featured an expensive, surreal music video.
Depeche Mode's 'Just Can't Get Enough' was written by Vince Clarke and released on September 7, 1981, becoming their first major hit.
The Oberheim DMX drum machine, used on records by New Order and Madonna, was one of the first digital drum machines with user-loadable sounds.
Pet Shop Boys' name was inspired by friends who worked in a pet shop in Ealing, West London.
Talking Heads' Tina Weymouth and Chris Frantz formed the side project Tom Tom Club, whose 'Genius of Love' was sampled by Mariah Carey and Grandmaster Flash.
The goth subculture that emerged from post-punk was heavily influenced by bands like Bauhaus, Siouxsie and the Banshees, and The Cure.
Gary Numan's 'Replicas' (1979) was one of the first concept albums about android identity and artificial intelligence in pop music.
Kate Bush's 'The Kick Inside' (1978) was released when she was just 19, and she had written many of its songs years earlier as a teenager.
Human League's 'Being Boiled' (1978) was one of the earliest synth-pop singles ever released, predating most of the genre's commercial breakthrough.
Blondie's Chris Stein was diagnosed with the rare autoimmune disease pemphigus in 1983, putting the band on hiatus until 1997.
The Cure released 'Wish' on April 21, 1992, debuting at number one in the UK charts.
Depeche Mode performed to over 80,000 fans at the Rose Bowl in 1988, proving that synth-pop could fill stadiums like traditional rock.
New Order's manager Rob Gretton and Factory Records' Tony Wilson had a famously contentious but productive working relationship.
OMD's Andy McCluskey later wrote songs for Atomic Kitten, including their number-one hit 'Whole Again.'
Magazine's Howard Devoto left Buzzcocks after their debut EP to form Magazine, citing creative differences over punk's musical limitations.
The Roland D-50 synthesizer, released in 1987, introduced LA synthesis and was used by Enya, Jean Michel Jarre, and many late-era new wave acts.
Erasure's 'Abba-esque' EP (1992), featuring covers of four ABBA songs, reached number one in the UK singles chart.
Siouxsie and the Banshees' 'Peepshow' (1988) expanded their sound with strings, flamenco guitar, and Middle Eastern influences.
The angular, jangly guitar sound popularized by new wave bands influenced the alt-rock and indie-pop movements of the 1990s.
Duran Duran's 'A View to a Kill' (1985) made them the first band to perform a James Bond theme that reached number one in the US.
Echo & the Bunnymen took their name from a conversation about the drum machine they used — they wanted the machine to be a 'member' called Echo.
Tears for Fears' 'Seeds of Love' (1989) took three years and roughly one million pounds to record, reflecting its complex, Beatles-influenced production.
The Berlin Wall fell on November 9, 1989, a moment closely associated with David Bowie's 'Heroes,' which referenced the Wall.
The Cure's 'Close to Me' (1985) featured an accordion and a music video by Tim Pope showing the band trapped inside a wardrobe pushed off a cliff.
New wave bands frequently used Simmons electronic drum kits, which produced a distinctive hexagonal pad sound that defined the era.
Gary Numan returned to critical acclaim in the 2010s with albums like 'Splinter' and 'Savage,' earning some of the best reviews of his career.
New Order were the recording name for the remaining members of Joy Division after Ian Curtis died in 1980.
Talking Heads' 'Fear of Music' (1979) and 'Remain in Light' (1980) were both produced by Brian Eno and are considered among the greatest art-rock albums.
Depeche Mode's 'Ultra' (1997) marked the band's comeback after Dave Gahan's near-fatal overdose and Martin Gore's alcohol dependency.
Soft Cell's Dave Ball later worked as a DJ and electronic music producer, involved in various projects including The Grid.
Kate Bush released 'Never for Ever' in 1980, becoming the first female solo artist to top the UK album chart.
The B-52s' 'Love Shack' (1989) became their biggest commercial hit, reaching number three on the US Billboard Hot 100.
The Cure's Simon Gallup quit the band in 1982 after a fight with Robert Smith, rejoining in 1984 and remaining since.
New Order's 'True Faith' (1987) was accompanied by a Philippe Decouflé-directed video featuring interpretive dancers in colorful costumes.
Ultravox's Billy Currie was a classically trained violinist who brought orchestral textures to the band's electronic sound.
Depeche Mode's 'Stripped' (1986) featured field recordings and industrial samples, showcasing their experimental approach to production.
INXS's 'Kick' (1987) sold over 20 million copies worldwide and contained hits like 'Need You Tonight,' 'Devil Inside,' and 'Never Tear Us Apart.'
Pet Shop Boys have won three Brit Awards and were named the most successful duo in UK music history by The Guinness Book of Records.
Bauhaus's 'In the Flat Field' (1980) was recorded at Beck Studios in Wellingborough and produced by 4AD founder Ivo Watts-Russell.
Blondie's Debbie Harry was born Angela Trimble on July 1, 1945, in Miami, Florida, and was adopted as an infant.
The LinnLM-1, predecessor to the LinnDrum, was the first drum machine to use digital samples of real drum sounds when released in 1980.
Tears for Fears' 'Shout' was inspired by primal scream therapy, with the title literally inviting listeners to release pent-up emotions.
Depeche Mode have played over 1,500 concerts across their career, with tours routinely selling out arenas and stadiums worldwide.
Howard Jones was born John Howard Jones on February 23, 1955, in Southampton, Hampshire, England.
The Cure's 'Fascination Street' from 'Disintegration' was partly inspired by a street in New Orleans that Robert Smith visited.
New Order's Haçienda nightclub in Manchester, co-owned by the band and Factory Records, became one of the most important venues in dance music history.
Cocteau Twins were signed to 4AD Records and never achieved mainstream chart success, yet they are considered one of the most innovative acts of the new wave era.
Yellow Magic Orchestra were among the first artists to use the Roland TR-808 drum machine, helping to popularize it globally.
Gang of Four's Jon King and Andy Gill met at the University of Leeds and were influenced by Situationist philosophy and art theory.
The Cure's 'Lullaby' (1989) featured a creepy spider-man video by Tim Pope, becoming one of the band's most visually memorable singles.
A-ha scored the theme song for the James Bond film 'The Living Daylights' in 1987, blending their synth-pop style with orchestral arrangements.
Depeche Mode's concert film '101' documented the final show of their 'Music for the Masses' tour, directed by D.A. Pennebaker and Chris Hegedus.
The new wave era saw the rise of the music video as an art form, with directors gaining recognition previously reserved for film directors.
Simple Minds' Jim Kerr was married to Chrissie Hynde of the Pretenders and later to actress Patsy Kensit.
Duran Duran's 'Ordinary World' (1992) marked a successful artistic comeback, shifting from glossy pop to more mature, introspective songwriting.
David Bowie's 'Let's Dance,' produced by Nile Rodgers, became his biggest commercial hit in 1983, though purists debated its pop direction.
New Order's 'Regret' (1993) became their highest-charting single in the US, reaching number 28 on the Billboard Hot 100.
Talking Heads expanded to a nine-piece band for their 1983 'Speaking in Tongues' tour, adding backup singers and extra musicians.
Kate Bush is one of the few artists in history to have had number-one UK singles in four different decades.
The Cure's 'Head on the Door' (1985) was their commercial breakthrough, containing the singles 'In Between Days' and 'Close to Me.'
Depeche Mode released 'Speak & Spell' on October 5, 1981, their debut album featuring Vince Clarke's catchy songwriting.
Nick Rhodes of Duran Duran has been an avid photographer and art collector throughout his career, curating exhibitions internationally.
Factory Records never made artists sign contracts; the label operated on handshake agreements and trust, a policy that led to legendary financial difficulties.
Pet Shop Boys' 'Go West' (1993) was a reimagined cover of the Village People classic, becoming a football stadium anthem across Europe.
Synthesizer pioneer Wendy Carlos released 'Switched-On Bach' in 1968, demonstrating the musical potential of the Moog synthesizer years before new wave emerged.
Siouxsie and the Banshees' 'Dear Prudence,' a Beatles cover, reached number three in the UK in 1983.
The Cure was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame on March 29, 2019.
Depeche Mode's 'Never Let Me Down Again' featured an ascending synth melody that became the band's signature concert closer.
Gary Numan influenced Nine Inch Nails' Trent Reznor, who called Numan 'one of the most important artists of the electronic age.'
New Order's 'Ceremony' was the last song written by Joy Division before Ian Curtis's death and became New Order's first single.
The Boss DD-3 digital delay pedal, released in 1986, became a staple effect for new wave and post-punk guitarists seeking ambient, spacious tones.
OMD's 'The Architecture & Morality' (1981) sold over four million copies and featured the hit singles 'Souvenir' and 'Joan of Arc.'
Cyndi Lauper's 'Time After Time' reached number one in the US in June 1984 and has been covered by Miles Davis, INOJ, and many others.
Soft Cell's Marc Almond suffered a near-fatal motorcycle accident in 2004 that left him in a coma for two weeks.
The influence of early video game sound chips, particularly the SID chip in the Commodore 64, has been noted as a parallel development to synth-pop.
Erasure's Vince Clarke built his career around simple, catchy synth melodies, proving that electronic music could be deeply emotional.
Blondie released 'Autoamerican' in 1980, which included the reggae-inspired 'The Tide Is High' and the rap-featuring 'Rapture.'
Human League's 'Fascination' (1983) continued the band's run of synth-pop hits, though they never quite replicated the success of 'Dare.'
New Order released 'Movement,' their debut album, on November 13, 1981, still heavily influenced by Joy Division's sound.
The Cure's 'Pictures of You' from 'Disintegration' was inspired by photographs of Robert Smith's wife that survived a house fire.
Gary Numan's 'Savage (Songs from a Broken World)' (2017) was a concept album about a post-apocalyptic desert Earth ravaged by climate change.
Duran Duran's Andy Taylor, John Taylor, and Roger Taylor are not related to each other despite sharing the same surname.
The Haçienda nightclub, co-owned by New Order, hosted legendary DJ sets by Graeme Park and Mike Pickering before closing in 1997.
Kate Bush built her own studio and became one of the first female artists to take complete creative control over her recording process.
Tears for Fears' 'Woman in Chains' featured Oleta Adams on vocals and Phil Collins on drums, creating a soulful, atmospheric ballad.
Pet Shop Boys directed and starred in a rarely seen 1988 film called 'It Couldn't Happen Here,' a surreal road movie.
The Berlin Wall's cultural significance made the city a magnet for artists like Bowie, Iggy Pop, and Nick Cave during the new wave era.
Joy Division's 'Atmosphere' features one of the most haunting synth melodies in post-punk, composed by Bernard Sumner on a Transcendent 2000 synthesizer kit.
CHVRCHES' Lauren Mayberry has spoken about the influence of Depeche Mode and The Cure on the band's sound and aesthetic.
New Order's 'Temptation' (1982) was one of the first tracks to meld indie guitar pop with electronic sequencing, foreshadowing their future direction.
Talking Heads' Chris Frantz and Tina Weymouth were married before the band formed, making them one of rock's most famous musical couples.
Duran Duran sold over 100 million records worldwide and were one of the best-selling bands of the 1980s.
The Cure's 'Three Imaginary Boys' (1979) was the band's debut album, released when Robert Smith was only 19 years old.
Depeche Mode's 'Construction Time Again' (1983) introduced industrial sampling and musique concrète techniques into mainstream synth-pop.
The B-52s returned from a five-year hiatus with 'Cosmic Thing' (1989), which included their biggest hits 'Love Shack' and 'Roam.'
Gary Numan made a cameo in the 1981 film 'Urgh! A Music War,' performing 'Down in the Park' alongside other new wave acts.
A-ha's 'Hunting High and Low' (1985) was the band's debut album and spent over 100 weeks on the Norwegian album chart.
The Cure performed a legendary set at Glastonbury Festival on June 29, 2019, playing for nearly three hours.
Billy Idol's 'Rebel Yell' (1983) featured guitarist Steve Stevens, whose virtuosic playing added a hard-rock edge to Idol's synth-punk sound.
Siouxsie and the Banshees disbanded in 1996 but reunited briefly for festival appearances in 2002.
The Emulator keyboard, first released in 1981, was a more affordable alternative to the Fairlight CMI and was used by Depeche Mode and New Order.
Depeche Mode's Alan Wilder now runs his own project called Recoil, releasing industrial-flavored electronic music.
Icehouse's 'Great Southern Land' (1982) is considered an unofficial Australian anthem, blending synth-pop with themes of national identity.
Echo & the Bunnymen's 'The Killing Moon' (1984) has been described by Ian McCulloch as the greatest song ever written.
Pet Shop Boys collaborated with the Pet Shop Boys Software company — actually, the Dusty Springfield collaboration was so successful it revived her career.
The new wave aesthetic influenced graphic design, with angular typefaces, bold colors, and geometric patterns appearing in album art and posters.
New Order's bassist Peter Hook opened a bar called FAC251 in the former Factory Records headquarters in Manchester.
Kraftwerk's 'Trans-Europe Express' (1977) directly inspired Afrika Bambaataa's 'Planet Rock,' bridging German electronic music and American hip-hop.
David Bowie was born David Robert Jones on January 8, 1947, in Brixton, London.
Depeche Mode's influence extends to genres from industrial and EBM to shoegaze and indie electronic, making them one of the most influential bands in modern music.
The Cure's 'Bloodflowers' (2000) was intended as the final part of a trilogy with 'Pornography' and 'Disintegration.'
Tears for Fears released 'The Tipping Point' in 2022, their first album of new material in nearly 18 years.
Heaven 17, formed from the original Human League lineup, scored a hit with 'Temptation' in 1983.
Blondie's 'Atomic' (1980) blended new wave with spaghetti western influences, featuring Morricone-inspired guitar textures.
New Order's 'Low-Life' (1985) balanced synthesizer-driven dance tracks like 'The Perfect Kiss' with guitar-based songs like 'Love Vigilantes.'
The Boss CE-1 Chorus Ensemble, released in 1976, was the first chorus effect pedal and helped define the shimmering guitar tone of new wave.
Pet Shop Boys' Chris Lowe almost never speaks in interviews, earning him the nickname 'the silent one.'
Gary Numan's 'Down in the Park' depicted a dystopian future where humans are hunted for sport by machines.
Duran Duran's 'Save a Prayer' featured an iconic music video shot in Sri Lanka, with misty landscapes and Buddhist temples.
The Cure's 'A Forest' (1980) is built around Simon Gallup's driving bass line and Robert Smith's hypnotic guitar arpeggios.
Depeche Mode performed at the Olympiastadion in Berlin on June 10, 2009, as part of their 'Tour of the Universe.'
The 4AD label's distinctive visual identity was largely created by designer Vaughan Oliver, whose work became as iconic as the music itself.
Simple Minds' 'New Gold Dream' (1982) is considered their artistic peak, blending synth-pop, post-punk, and ambient textures.
The influence of German Krautrock bands like Can, Neu!, and Tangerine Dream on new wave and post-punk was profound and wide-reaching.
Kate Bush's 'Cloudbusting' video starred Donald Sutherland and was inspired by the relationship between Wilhelm Reich and his son Peter.
New Order's 'Blue Monday' was programmed primarily by Stephen Morris and Gillian Gilbert using an Oberheim DMX and Emulator keyboard.
Talking Heads' 'Life During Wartime' was about surviving in a dystopian urban environment, referencing guerrilla warfare and paranoia.
The Smithsonian National Museum of American History has a Roland TR-808 in its collection, recognizing its cultural significance.
Depeche Mode's 'People Are People' (1984) addressed prejudice and intolerance, becoming one of their first major international hits.
The new wave and post-punk scenes provided a platform for many female artists and musicians who were underrepresented in punk's male-dominated world.
Erasure's 'Chains of Love' (1988) showcased Andy Bell's powerful voice over Vince Clarke's signature bright synthesizer arrangements.
Billy Idol's 'Eyes Without a Face' (1984) was a synth-driven ballad that reached number four on the US Billboard Hot 100.
The Cure released their album 'Wish' which contained 'Friday I'm in Love,' one of their most cheerful and commercially successful songs.
Duran Duran's Roger Taylor retired from the band in 1985 due to exhaustion and the pressures of fame, later returning in 2001.
Joy Division's 'Unknown Pleasures' was released on June 15, 1979, on Factory Records.
The Cure's 'In Between Days' was inspired by a brief romantic encounter, and its jangly guitar riff became one of the most recognized in alternative music.
The synthwave genre of the 2010s took direct visual and sonic inspiration from 1980s synth-pop, John Carpenter film scores, and VHS-era aesthetics.
Depeche Mode's 'Memento Mori' (2023) was their first album without founding member Andy Fletcher, who died on May 26, 2022.
The Thompson Twins, despite their name, were a trio from Sheffield who scored hits like 'Hold Me Now' and 'Doctor! Doctor!' in the mid-1980s.
Fad Gadget, the stage name of Frank Tovey, was the first artist signed to Mute Records and a major influence on Depeche Mode's early sound.
The Roland SH-2 synthesizer was used by Martin Gore of Depeche Mode on early tracks, contributing to their raw, buzzy synth texture.
Alphaville's 'Forever Young' (1984) became one of synth-pop's most enduring anthems, later covered by Jay-Z and used in numerous films.
The Psychedelic Furs blended post-punk guitar noise with pop songwriting; their song 'Pretty in Pink' inspired the 1986 John Hughes film.
Visage, fronted by Steve Strange, epitomized the New Romantic movement with their 1980 hit 'Fade to Grey.'
Modern English's 'I Melt with You' (1982) became a staple of 1980s new wave cinema soundtracks, appearing in 'Valley Girl' and later films.
The Roland CR-78 was one of the earliest programmable drum machines and was used by Blondie on 'Heart of Glass' and Phil Collins on 'In the Air Tonight.'
Yazoo's 'Don't Go' (1982) showcased Alison Moyet's powerful blues-influenced voice against Vince Clarke's pulsing synth arrangements.
Spandau Ballet emerged from London's Blitz club scene in 1979, becoming one of the most visible New Romantic bands alongside Duran Duran.
The Eurythmics, the duo of Annie Lennox and Dave Stewart, blended synth-pop with soul and achieved massive success with 'Sweet Dreams (Are Made of This)' in 1983.
Cabaret Voltaire, from Sheffield, blended tape manipulation, electronics, and industrial noise to help pioneer the electronic body music genre.
Book of Love, an American synth-pop band from New York, drew heavily from European new wave and were signed to Sire Records.
Depeche Mode's 'Precious' (2005) was their first single released after the departure of Andrew Fletcher from active touring duties temporarily.
The Buggles' 'Video Killed the Radio Star' was not only the first video on MTV but also a synth-pop classic co-produced by Trevor Horn.
